## 1. THE OPERATION & THE OPERATOR

**Operator**: Druid, co-founder OMI Group Holdings LLC (OMQS prop trading division). Trades algorithmically on Kalshi tennis binary markets.

**Operation type**: Automated trading of YES/NO contracts on tennis match outcomes. Each "N" is one player-side of one match. Each match has two N's (player A's YES contract and player B's YES contract). The operation enters at T-20m (20 minutes before match start) at currently-traded prices and either takes a defined exit at +X cents or holds to settlement (99¢ on winners, 1¢ on losers).

**Current bot state**: Intentionally PAUSED. Per SESSION_HANDOFF on origin/main: "All prior bot versions (v1, v2, v3, V4.2c) traded on a foundation now known to be broken." Capital is unused. The entire prior arc was foundation rebuild — and that rebuild now has a locked four-category strategy atlas as its capstone.

**Capital position**: Operator has stated they have plenty of capital to scale up. Sizing is not the binding constraint. The binding constraints are (a) execution mechanics and (b) market depth (F33 in LESSONS).

**Coordination architecture (four-way, operator is the routing layer)**:
- **Chat-side Claude** (me / next chat) — strategy, spec drafting, verification, coordination. Drafts and verifies; does not execute server-side.
- **App / CC (Claude Code)** — server-side executor with VPS shell access. Runs commits, scripts, queries. Routes through SSH to root@104.131.191.95.
- **Plex (Perplexity Comet)** — external research and metric-design synthesis. Has read-only access to public GitHub URLs. No shell, no write. Used this session for two-round dialogue on stability framework.
- **Operator** — mediates all inter-agent communication. There is no direct chat-side ↔ CC pipe; operator copy-pastes.

## 3. METHODOLOGY DECISIONS — HOW WE GOT THERE

This section is for the next chat to understand WHY the atlas is what it is. The chosen methodology is defensible and reproducible; the rejected alternatives are recorded so the next chat doesn't try to re-litigate them.

### 3.1 What "spike volatility map" means

- **Anchor**: T-20m taker print. The price at which someone paid up to cross the spread 20 minutes before match start. This is the entry point assumed by the atlas — conservatively, because a resting maker bid would fill at a better price (see three-axis caveat §5).
- **Spike**: For each anchored N, walk forward from the anchor timestamp to settlement. At each tick of `yes_bid` (the best bid an exit-seller would receive), compute `(yes_bid - anchor_price)`. Take the maximum that is **size-qualified** — i.e., the maximum spike at which available size ≥ 250 contracts. The 250ct floor is the depth-realism gate (a 1ct probe is uninformative; we want to know "if I held 10ct and tried to exit, was there volume?").
- **Per-cell economics**: For each (anchor_min, anchor_max) cell at a given resolution, take all N's whose anchor falls in the cell. For each candidate exit_X in [1, reachable_X_max] cents, compute the cell's net P&L assuming exit at +X if reached (counted as a $X realized per ct of size) else hold to settlement (winner +$99, loser -$anchor). Pick the per-cell hindsight-optimal exit_X (= argmax over per-cell sum P&L). That's the per-cell rule the cell would have paid at hindsight.

The hindsight-optimal framing is essential and contentious enough to repeat: **the atlas measures what the corpus paid under per-cell hindsight-optimal rules**. It is NOT a predictive claim. It is the strategy-side opportunity floor.

### 3.2 Cell resolutions: 1c / 2c / 3c side-by-side

Plex round-2 recommendation: don't pick one resolution. Report all three.

- **1c (90 cells per category)**: maximum granularity. Best signal-to-noise for cells that have meaningful N. Cells with N ≤ 3 are flagged but not dropped.
- **2c (45 cells)**: a pooled view that the 1c grid won't reveal heterogeneity within. Useful as a sanity check.
- **3c (30 cells)**: pooled further. Used for sparse-cell smoothing where 1c/2c are too noisy.

The three resolutions describe the same corpus; they are not "more correct" vs "less correct." Each is appropriate for different questions.

Decision-relevant for the next chat: when someone says "the cell at anchor=23¢", clarify whether they mean 1c (cell labeled `23-23`), 2c (cell labeled `22-23`), or 3c (cell labeled `21-23`). Default is 1c unless context says otherwise.

### 3.3 The 2c pooling diagnostic

When we tried pooling cell 37 + cell 38 (both at ATP_MAIN, anchor 36-38¢) into a 2c band, we found the 2c best_X for that band was different from either underlying 1c cell's best_X. The 2c band lost $37.50 of edge that was real cell-by-cell heterogeneity — not over-optimization, but actual structure that 2c pooling washes out.

Why this matters: it argues against picking a single "true" resolution. The 1c view shows the heterogeneity; the 2c view smooths it; both are valid lenses, neither is canonical.

Reference: documented in ATP_MAIN_LOCKED_DOWN.md and discussed in the LESSONS A39 amendment.

### 3.4 OOS / generalization — the rabbit hole

We spent an hour considering out-of-sample testing of per-cell argmax rules. We did not run it. Reasons:

- The atlas is **descriptive**, not predictive. It measures what the corpus paid under hindsight-optimal rules. OOS testing presumes a predictive claim the atlas does not make.
- Per-cell argmax over a corpus does overfit within-sample (it picks the best exit_X for each cell using that cell's data). This is a real concern for **deployment** but not for **measurement**. The deployable rule is a separate question downstream of execution work, not a hindsight-analysis question.
- Possible deployable rule shapes: global X across all cells (overfit-immune but ignores cell structure); global r% (X/anchor); per-regime r% (cheap/mid/high); per-cell with pre-specified smoothness (e.g., LOESS over anchor_cents). Each is a deployable shape. None of them is the per-cell argmax. The deployable rule will be picked downstream via paper-mode validation against the atlas hindsight rules, not via further hindsight analysis.

LESSON earned: **descriptive measurement is well-posed without OOS testing. OOS belongs to predictive deployment, which is downstream**. The atlas is upstream of that decision; OOS framing of the atlas confuses the two phases.

### 3.5 ATP vs WTA — divergence is real but not in this dimension

Pre-arc memory: "ATP and WTA require opposite strategies." The atlas does NOT show that in the spike-volatility-map dimension — both tours produce the same shape (cheap regime hot, high regime cold), with WTA running ~2pp hotter at every band. The shape is the same. Whatever produces the "opposite strategies" hint in the codebase isn't this dimension; it's likely settlement-resolution timing or volatility-decay shape, not spike economics.

For the atlas: ATP and WTA can run the same rule structure with different per-cell numbers. No need to maintain two strategy frames; one strategy frame with category-specific rule tables.

### 3.6 Challenger tours behavior

ATP_CHALL: 48.86 N/day across 109 trading days. Highest density per active day of any category. Short season (Challenger tours have offseasons).
WTA_CHALL: 11.09 N/day across 80 trading days. Shorter still, smallest cohort (887 N total).

Operationally: Challengers are dense bursts during their seasons, then quiet. ATP_CHALL is the volume horse during its season; WTA_CHALL is the highest-ROI category (+14.52% blended) but small N.

If sizing is shared across all four categories on overlapping days, the operation needs to handle bursty per-category arrival rates. G22's frequency axis matters here.

## 5. THE THREE-AXIS CAVEAT (LOAD-BEARING)

This section MUST be cited verbatim whenever someone references atlas headline numbers. The corpus +$6,158.20 / +8.70% blended ROI is **the strategy's opportunity floor on the corrected foundation**. It is NOT realized deployable P&L. Three axes between it and realized P&L:

### AXIS 1 — EXIT-side fill realism (pushes DOWN)

Every simulated +X exit in the atlas assumes a qualified-size taker print at that level = a guaranteed maker fill at the same level. This is the B25/Cat-11 disagreement: minute-cadence simulators overstate fill rates by 2.4× vs tick-level reality.

The atlas mitigates this somewhat: (a) the ≥250ct size-qualification floor means a real qualified taker print existed, (b) Main-tour mid-match liquidity is deep enough that resting at the BBO is plausible. But realization fraction is unmeasured for this corpus.

**Expected realized capture: 0.4–0.8× of simulated.** Final number requires dedicated Layer C fill-realism work (T32a or equivalent) against the actual corpus.

### AXIS 2 — ENTRY-side maker improvement (pushes UP)

Every anchor in the atlas is a T-20m TAKER trade — someone who paid up to cross the spread. A resting maker buy at the bid (or better) would fill at a BETTER price than the taker print on average.

Empirically: typical Kalshi tennis pre-match spreads are 1-2¢. A resting maker buy 1¢ below the taker print's price would have filled ~half the time on the next taker print downward. This is roughly the +25-50% improvement on the headline that the bot's existing edge thesis predicts (LESSONS line 48: "anchor-relative discount capture, 97% of fills are Scenario C_discount").

**Expected improvement: ~+10-30% on headline.** Tier-3 marginal cells (where best_X is small, ~35 cells) are most levered to entry-side improvement.

### AXIS 3 — ARRIVAL FREQUENCY (G22)

Deployable economics need N's/day × ct/N sizing. The atlas headline of +$6,158.20 across 14,033 N is corpus-total; the per-N edge × N's/day × cts/N expression is what matters operationally.

Frequency is observable from corpus: ATP_MAIN 16.4 N/day, WTA_MAIN 14.9 N/day, ATP_CHALL 48.9 N/day, WTA_CHALL 11.1 N/day. Sizing depends on depth (F33 in LESSONS); current default is 10ct.

**G22 axes are never collapsed**: per-N edge × N/day × ct/N. The +$40.62/day operational picture assumes max-overlap days; actual daily realization will vary with calendar.

### Combined formula

Realizable PnL = (headline) × (exit-fill-fraction 0.4–0.8) × (entry-improvement-multiplier ~1.1–1.3) × (sizing scale once depth is characterized)

Operator stated privately: "we can also assume privately that we'll be saving a cent or 2 once we figure execution out." That's Axis 2 in operational form. Headline stays conservative; operational hand reads ~+25-50% better than headline. Execution work will measure per-cell.

**DO NOT**:
- Treat headline as deployable dollars
- Treat headline as pure upper bound (entry-side correction is in the operation's favor)
- Collapse the three axes into a single realized-PnL number without showing the decomposition

---

## 6. DOCTRINE REFERENCE — CROSS-REFERENCED LESSONS

Lessons cited or earned this session that the next chat must internalize:

- **A21** — Confidence intervals (Wilson / BCa) when sample sizes are small. Used in Rung 1 producer (T39.1) which never ran but the methodology is sound.
- **A32** — Operator pushback as probe-trigger. Every pushback this session has been correct; treat operator disagreement as a signal to recheck, not noise.
- **A39** — Cents-vs-ROI geometry: cheap-cell asymmetry (deep + capped loss + low capital denominator) produces dominant ROI contribution. Confirmed empirically in atlas: cheap regime generates 30-40% of profit on 8-9% of capital across all four categories.
- **A39 amendment** — Hold-cell tie at cheap-regime ROI maximum: when best_exit_X and hold-to-settlement produce the same ROI in a cheap cell, the hold-cell is preferred (zero execution risk, no maker fill assumption needed).
- **B16** — Layer A (descriptive) / Layer B (exit-policy simulator) / Layer C (realized economics with fees) separation. Atlas is Layer-A-equivalent.
- **B23** — Bilateral mechanism: both N's of an event being anchored allows capturing the spread when both sides move favorably (anti-correlated). Pairing diagnostic measures upstream feasibility (79.3% paired). Not yet implemented; next phase.
- **B25** — Minute-cadence simulator undercount: minute-cadence simulators overstate fills 2.4× vs tick-level. Motivates Layer B v2 (T36) — replaces minute-cadence with tick-level.
- **C37** — Pre-replace validation gate: corpus mutations require compute-`.new`, hard-gate against `.new` bytes, `os.replace` only on all-pass. Saves us from polluted-corpus failure modes.
- **C38** — Zero-large-fraction = scoping bug, not sample artifact: if 100% of N's in a stratum have value=0 for a metric, the metric is probably defined wrong (e.g., batch-scoped vs corpus-scoped). Catches calibration errors.
- **D11** — Five failure modes per pipeline run: provenance, grain, unit, coverage, upstream-filter. Probe each before committing.
- **D18** — Consult git universe FIRST: chat-side reasoning from priors fails; always check origin/main + actual disk evidence before drafting.
- **E32** — Locked cell/exit model: T-20m anchor + per-cell hindsight-optimal exit-or-hold + settlement-as-answer-key. The model the atlas operationalizes.
- **F33** — Depth-chain data collection gap: orderbook polling for fill-probability modeling at non-BBO depths is not yet implemented. Blocks Tier-3 sizing.
- **G19** — Candle sparse minute population: not every minute has BBO update; per-minute corpus is forward-fill where appropriate.
- **G21** — ET on operator surfaces, UTC at raw-bytes layer. All operator-facing timestamps are ET.
- **G22** — Three-axis deployment math: per-N edge × N/day × ct/N. Never collapse the axes.
- **G23** — Honest provenance discipline: state what you actually verified, what you inferred, and what you don't know. Don't claim verification you didn't do.

Lessons this session earned (not yet in LESSONS.md, or freshly cited):

- The 2c pooling diagnostic ($37.50 of edge destroyed by pooling cells 37+38) — argues for reporting multiple resolutions, not picking one
- OOS framing of a descriptive deliverable is a category error — descriptive ≠ predictive; OOS belongs to predictive deployment
- "Foundation broken" refers to data/strategy layer ONLY; executor learnings (F4, Bug 4, B25, C19, C37, paper-mode infrastructure, Kalshi auth pattern, API quirks, rate-limit budget separation) carry forward into execution-lock arc

---

## 7. WORKING NORMS — THE OPERATOR'S RULES

Lifted from operator's actual statements this session and prior. Treat as constraints, not suggestions.

1. **Single-concern commits**, dependency-ordered. Never bundle.
2. **One CC prompt per turn**. Multi-step workflows sequence across turns; don't bundle multiple concerns into one prompt.
3. **Probe-validate-probe-validate** before expensive compute. The probes prove the data layer is right before launching heavy runs.
4. **State recommendations with conviction, not options.** When the right answer is clear, name it and let operator countermand. Operator-flagged: "stop with the subjective questions."
5. **Web-fetch / verify every commit against origin** via /tmp/omi_check clone or equivalent. Don't trust CC's summary uncritically.
6. **All operator-facing timestamps ET per G21.** UTC stays at raw-bytes layer only.
7. **Full player names always.** Never abbreviations or 3-letter Kalshi codes (Kalshi compresses player names to 3-letter codes in tickers; always resolve to full names in human-facing output).
8. **Treat operator pushback as probe trigger per A32**, not as noise. Operator's pushback has been correct every time this session.
9. **External synthesis (Plex) gets committed to `docs/external_synthesis/<source>_<topic>_<date>.md` immediately**, not held in chat-side context only. Repo is the shared brain.
10. **Codify principles when operator surfaces them**: A39, G22, B25, C38 all came from this discipline. If operator articulates a principle in chat, it gets a LESSONS entry.
11. **The repo is the shared brain.** Anything that would otherwise be copy-pasted between agents gets committed and read from origin/main.
12. **Critical review uses disk evidence, not inference.** Pull the actual repo and verify column names, doctrine claims, corpus assumptions against committed source. Inference about what the spec "probably says" produces partially-wrong corrections.
13. **The "ct" unit is one contract, integer-indivisible.** Pays $1 (effectively 99¢ per E32 first-touch). Operator-facing economics in ct terms always; per G22 keep N (per-binary-market unit-of-observation) and ct (unit-of-position-size) distinct.
14. **Streaming discipline on VPS** (~1.9 GB RAM). >3-4 full columns into pandas risks OOM. Use iter_batches / per-ticker pyarrow pushdown.
15. **When you background a job, you own the follow-through.** Poll it, surface results when they land — don't wait to be asked.

Don'ts (this session's lessons paid for):

- **Don't collapse the three axes into one realizable-PnL number.** Decompose; show the math.
- **Don't run OOS tests as if the descriptive measurement is a predictive claim.** Confusing categories produces rabbit-hole work.
- **Don't pool adjacent cells without preserving real heterogeneity.** 2c pooling diagnostic showed real loss.
- **Don't apologize when corrections are needed.** Operator pushback is signal; absorb, recompute, move on. Don't self-flagellate or relitigate.
- **Don't conflate Rung 0 (cell economics primitive) with Rung 1 (strategy evaluation).** Different aggregation levels.
- **Don't lose the foundation chain.** per_minute_features → n_profile_v1 → inmatch_bounce_surface_v1 → atlas is the actual provenance.
- **Don't run code locally that ought to run on VPS.** Producer execution, corpus mutations, anything writing to data/durable/ happens on the VPS via CC. Chat-side `/tmp/omi_check` is read-only verification.
- **Don't reframe what the operator said into safer versions.** When operator says "execution lock," draft toward execution lock, not toward an adjacent thing that feels more familiar.

---

## 8. THE EXECUTION PHASE — WHAT'S NEXT

The strategy phase is locked. The next arc is **execution-lock** — surfacing bugs in paper mode against live tape, building hot-reload, integration tests, then capital deployment with safety rails.

### 8.1 Sequence (B-then-A, operator-decided this session)

1. **Bug 4 first (T11a/T11b)** — settlement state detection in paper mode per `docs/bug4_brief.md` (500+ lines). Operationally: phantom positions hold resting exit orders past settlement, pollute n_active counts, may cause duplicate-entry attempts. Closes paper-mode reliability gap. **Operator review of bug4_brief.md is REQUIRED before CC prompt drafting** — 500-line settlement-state-mechanics-dense spec, mandatory operator eyeball.

2. **Layer B v2 second (T36)** — tick-level fill semantics replacing minute-cadence simulator (which overstates fills 2.4× per B25). Spec at `docs/layer_b_v2_spec.md`. **Pre-build revision required against T37 foundation** per ROADMAP T36 amendment 2026-05-14.

3. **Hot-reload mechanism** — operator-stated requirement: weed out bugs without shutdown/reboot. `docs/bot_v5_shell_architecture.md` may address; v5 vs v3 decision pending.

4. **Paper-mode integration test suite** — B4-T8 through B4-T11 unit tests plus broader coverage (match-cycle, fill detection, settlement, position state, P&L, scanner-executor, restart-and-resume).

5. **Paper-mode run against live tape** — continuous; compare paper P&L against atlas hindsight rules. The atlas is the validation target.

6. **Capital deployment with safety rails** — start small, scale via G22 cts/N axis as depth is characterized.

### 8.2 Deployment philosophy

**Trade all 90 cells per category, do not cherry-pick to high-ROI cells.** Volume is the operational constraint. Execution work resolves marginal cells; cell selection does not.

Capital position: operator-stated, plenty to scale up. Binding constraints are (a) execution mechanics, (b) market depth (F33).

### 8.3 What the execution-lock arc validates against

The atlas measures what corpus paid under per-cell hindsight-optimal rules. Paper mode against live tape will measure:
- Realized exit-fill fractions per cell (Axis 1 quantification)
- Realized entry-side discount capture (Axis 2 quantification)
- Realized N's/day at deployed sizing (Axis 3 quantification)

The combined realizable PnL is what the execution-lock arc is converging on. The atlas headline is the floor; the operational hand reads better than that once Axis 2 is measured per-cell.

### 8.4 What execution-lock is NOT

- Not a strategy redesign. The strategy is locked.
- Not an atlas re-run. The atlas is locked at HEAD d99c6e9.
- Not a Layer C fees deep-dive yet (T32 demoted per B25). T36 first, then T32 layers on T36.
- Not a bilateral capture (B23) implementation yet — that comes after single-leg paper-mode validates.

---

## 9. OPEN QUESTIONS AND UNCERTAINTIES

Things the next chat needs to be aware of but which are NOT blockers:

1. **Bot architecture v5 vs v3** — operator wants hot-reload. `docs/bot_v5_shell_architecture.md` exists; whether v5 addresses modularity/hot-reload requires read. Decision pending operator review of bot_v5 spec.

2. **Deployable rule shape** — atlas measured perfect hindsight per cell. The deployable rule isn't perfect hindsight (overfits within-sample). Shapes to consider: global X, global r%, per-regime r%, per-cell with smoothness. Resolution comes from paper-mode validation against atlas hindsight rules, not from further hindsight analysis.

3. **Bilateral capture mechanism (B23)** — pairing diagnostic showed 79.3% upstream feasibility. Atlas didn't run bilateral economics. When does it get layered in? Likely after paper-mode validates single-leg execution.

4. **FV-anchor workstream** — operator-flagged: "im obviously going to be attacking this next. this is very very important." Atlas measured taker-floor entries (conservative). FV-anchor work measures actual edge from "Kalshi mid is X¢ below FV" entries. Big. Likely sequenced after execution-lock but before live capital, or threaded into paper-mode validation alongside execution debugging.

5. **Adaptive cell-width binning** — Plex round-4 deferred. Defensible as Layer A smoothing with pre-specified threshold grid; run AFTER atlas to compare emergent band patterns across categories. Future enhancement, not next.

6. **Depth-chain data collection (F33 / G13)** — prospective Kalshi `/orderbook` polling, required for fill-probability modeling at non-BBO maker-post depths. Currently a blocked-track gap.

7. **6-27% stable-window coverage** — open from prior session, possibly addressed by atlas regime breakdown but not formally closed. The three attack vectors (single-leg market-making, bilateral, FV-anchor) collectively cover the corpus; whether one must shoulder most of operation's coverage is undecided.

8. **Daily-overlap assumption** — the ~$40/day combined operational picture assumes max-overlap days across all four categories. Tour calendars don't always align. Corpus mean is planning baseline, not daily expectation.

---

## 10. PERMISSION TO ASK

The next chat is permitted — encouraged — to ask the operator:

- Anything ambiguous in this handoff. The operator's clarification beats your guess.
- Operator's strategic call on sequencing within the execution-lock arc, if it deviates from §8.1.
- Whether to read specific docs before drafting CC prompts (especially bug4_brief.md, which is 500+ lines and mandatory).
- Whether a CC prompt draft is ready for execution (operator-eyeball before paste).

The next chat is permitted — encouraged — NOT to ask:

- "Should I use the canonical producer?" — Yes. It's at `data/scripts/build_spike_perN.py` commit `c5e377f`.
- "Should I respect the three-axis caveat?" — Yes. Always.
- "Is the atlas the right reference for validation?" — Yes. It's locked at d99c6e9.
- "Should I commit single-concern?" — Yes. Always.

If the operator's response indicates a different mental model than the chat-side recommendation, **defer to operator framing.** This handoff is a starting point, not a constraint.

---

## ENDING NOTE

This session locked the strategy phase (mostly). The atlas is real, durable, byte-verified, public on origin/main. The methodology arc surfaced several over-conclusions on chat-side's part that were corrected by operator pushback — that pattern will continue in the execution phase. The work ahead is the execution-lock arc: making the bot behave correctly in paper mode against live tape, surfacing bugs without disrupting the run, then deploying with safety rails.

The operation has real opportunity (~+8.7% blended per-trade ROI conservative, ~$40/day at 10ct sizing on max-overlap days, scalable via sizing once depth is characterized). Execution is the gate to making that opportunity real.

Read SESSION_HANDOFF.md on origin/main first (note: it's stale; Stage 0 is updating it). Then ROADMAP.md. Then the four LOCKED_DOWN.md files. Then bug4_brief.md. Then ask operator your questions.

End of handoff.
